title: Global citizenship and sustainable development education
subtitle: An index from 0 (worst) to 1 (best) measuring the extent to which global citizenship and sustainable development education are mainstreamed into different strands of education: national education policies, student assessment, teacher education, and education curricula.
caption:
Global citizenship and sustainable development education

An index from 0 (worst) to 1 (best) measuring the extent to which global citizenship and sustainable development education are mainstreamed into different strands of education: national education policies, student assessment, teacher education, and education curricula.

This chart presents 2020 index scores for four countries—Colombia, India, Malawi, and Bulgaria—measuring the mainstreaming of global citizenship and sustainable development education across four specific strands. The index ranges from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating greater integration. In Colombia, national education policies and student assessment both reach the maximum score of 1, while education curricula and teacher education score 0.88 and 0.85 respectively. India similarly shows scores of 1 for national education policies and student assessment, with teacher education at 0.95 and education curricula at 0.92. Malawi also records perfect scores of 1 for national education policies and student assessment, followed by 0.91 for education curricula and 0.9 for teacher education. Bulgaria displays lower scores across all categories: teacher education leads at 0.73, student assessment follows at 0.71, education curricula sits at 0.65, and national education policies register the lowest value at 0.56. All four countries show their highest scores in either national education policies or student assessment, while teacher education and education curricula tend to score slightly lower, except in India where teacher education is nearly maximal. The indicators are based on self-reporting by countries, though countries are asked to provide supporting evidence to back up their responses. The visual layout groups each country’s four bars together, allowing direct comparison within and across nations. No country falls below 0.56, and three of the four achieve the top score in at least two categories.
